title: Rotor Syndrome -- Introduction
content: Rotors syndrome (also known as Rotor type hyperbilirubinemia) is an autosomal recessive disease and a rare cause of mixed direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia. The disease is characterized by non-hemolytic jaundice due to chronic elevation of predominantly conjugated bilirubin (over 50%). This phenomenon results from impaired hepatocellular storage of conjugated bilirubin that leaks into plasma, causing hyperbilirubinemia. Its presenting symptom is jaundice, but Rotor Syndrome is a benign and self-limiting disorder that does not require treatment. [1] [2] Generally, patients are asymptomatic, and jaundice is usually an incidental finding. Regarding symptoms, it is similar to Dubin-Johnson syndrome; however, the liver is histologically normal.

title: Bilirubinuria -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Bilirubinuria is the presence of bilirubin in the urine, usually detected while performing a routine urine dipstick test. Its presence is abnormal and can be the first clinical pointer of serious underlying hepatobiliary disorder even before clinical jaundice is appreciated. This activity reviews the evaluation and management of patients presenting with bilirubinuria and highlights the interprofessional team approach in evaluating and treating this condition.

content: Referrals are indicated in cases of unexplained and persistent elevation of bilirubin alone or combination with AST, ALT, and alkaline phosphatase; typically, total bilirubin greater than 3 mg/dL, aminotransferases two times and alkaline phosphatase 1.5 times the upper limit of normal are considered significant. Additionally, if multiple etiologies are suspected, specialist referral is advantageous.

title: Rotor Syndrome -- Continuing Education Activity
content: Rotor syndrome, also known as Rotor type hyperbilirubinemia, is an autosomal recessive disease and a rare cause of mixed direct (conjugated) and indirect (unconjugated) hyperbilirubinemia. The disease is characterized by non-hemolytic jaundice due to chronic elevation of predominantly conjugated bilirubin. This phenomenon results from impaired hepatocellular storage of conjugated bilirubin that leaks into plasma, causing hyperbilirubinemia. Its presenting symptom is jaundice, but Rotor syndrome is a benign, self-limiting disorder that does not require treatment. This activity describes the pathophysiology and presentation of rotor syndrome and highlights the role of the interprofessional team in its management.

title: Rotor Syndrome -- Evaluation
content: Rotor syndrome is largely a diagnosis of exclusion. Serological abnormalities in Rotor syndrome only include elevated total serum bilirubin (typically elevated between 2 to 5 mg/dL but may be as high as 20 mg/dL). Most of the time, alanine aminotransferase, aspartate aminotransferase, gamma-glutamyl transferase, and alkaline phosphatase levels are normal, but mild elevations can be seen. If any of these lab values are markedly elevated, investigation for other, more serious conditions is warranted. Imaging studies cannot diagnose Rotor syndrome but can help rule out other diseases that cause hyperbilirubinemia. For example, an ultrasound of the liver and biliary tree can help investigate the causes of extra-hepatic biliary obstruction. The gallbladder is visualized on oral cholecystography in Rotor syndrome, while it is not visualized in DJS. [19] Ultimately, the best method of diagnosing the disease is the analysis of urine coproporphyrin excretion. The total urine coproporphyrin excretion in Rotor syndrome has a two to five-fold elevation, with 65% constituting coproporphyrin 1. [2] [17]

title: Rotor Syndrome -- Introduction
content: As opposed to Dubin-Johnson syndrome, liver hyperpigmentation is absent in Rotor syndrome. In addition, the serum bilirubin in Gilbert syndrome is mostly unconjugated, unlike in Rotor syndrome. Rotor syndrome generally begins shortly after birth or during childhood. Jaundice could be intermittent, and conjunctival icterus could be the only clinical manifestation. The manifestation of jaundice/icterus is because of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, excess coproporphyrin in urine, and near-absent liver uptake of anionic diagnostics, including cholescintigraphic tracers. [3]

title: Gilbert Syndrome -- Differential Diagnosis -- Conjugated Hyperbilirubinemia
content: Differential diagnoses that should be considered with various causes of conjugated hyperbilirubinemia include: Defects of canalicular organic anion transport :  Dubin-Johnson syndrome Defects of sinusoidal reuptake of conjugated bilirubin :  Rotor syndrome Extrahepatic cholestasis: Choledocholithiasis, pancreaticobiliary malignancy, primary sclerosing cholangitis, pancreatitis, parasitic infection Intrahepatic cholestasis :  Viral hepatitis, alcoholic liver disease, non-alcoholic fatty liver disease, primary biliary cholangitis, drugs and toxins, sepsis, infiltrative diseases, parenteral nutrition, sickle cell disease, pregnancy, end-stage liver disease [45]

title: Rotor Syndrome -- History and Physical
content: Physical examination is typically normal, except for mild jaundice. Excess bilirubin results in a yellow-orange tinge of the tissues that most easily appears as icteric (yellowish) discoloration of the sclera. Unlike other disorders with cholestasis, pruritus is absent. Another feature to remember in Rotor syndrome is that there will be no hepatosplenomegaly in the presence of jaundice which can help narrow down the list of differentials.

title: Jaundice -- Pearls and Other Issues
content: The best initial step in the evaluation of jaundice is history and physical examination. The next step is to classify jaundice by etiology and type-Hepatocellular, cholestatic or mixed. The basic mechanisms of jaundice include elevated production, decreased uptake, and faulty conjugation. Treatment focuses on the cause.

title: Jaundice -- Evaluation
content: AST/ALT ratio is more than 2 to 1 in alcoholic liver disease. AST and ALT values are in 1000s; then the hepatocellular disease is likely due to toxins like acetaminophen or ischemia or viral. If ALP levels are five times elevated than normal and liver transaminases are normal or less than two times normal, then the most likely cause is biliary obstruction. The high serum ALP levels due to a biliary injury can be differentiated from bone disorders by ordering a GGT serum profile, increased levels confirm hepatic origin. If AST, ALT and ALP levels are normal- then the jaundice is not due to liver or bile duct injury. The cause must probably be pre-hepatic: inherited disorders of liver conjugation or blood disorders or defect in hepatic excretion (Rotor, Dubin-Johnson). Serum Bilirubin - whether there is a rise in unconjugated or conjugated bilirubin

title: Cholestatic Jaundice  -- Evaluation -- Liver Chemistry Panel
content: Serum total and fractioned direct bilirubin should be obtained. A predominantly direct hyperbilirubinemia (more than 50% of total bilirubin) is observed in cholestasis. Serum alkaline phosphatase is often elevated 3 times greater than the upper standard limit of normal, while the hepatic transaminases may be normal or only mildly elevated. Serum albumin is usually normal except in cirrhosis and late chronic liver disease.

title: Rotor Syndrome -- Etiology
content: In a normal liver, a majority of bilirubin is conjugated by hepatocytes and secreted back into the blood. It is then reabsorbed in downstream hepatocytes by the OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 proteins. [6] In Rotor syndrome, the OATP1B1 and OATP1B3 proteins are abnormally short; therefore, the bilirubin is less efficiently taken up by the liver and removed from the body, causing a buildup of bilirubin in the blood and urine, which results in jaundice and dark urine. [7] [8] [9] [10]

title: Unconjugated Hyperbilirubinemia -- History and Physical
content: Patients with unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia may present with jaundice or other signs, or the condition may be noticed in the asymptomatic patient by the finding of high levels of bilirubin in the serum during routine blood testing. As far as the differential diagnosis of unconjugated hyperbilirubinemia is involved, it requires a detailed history and physical examination of patients for the evaluation. The assessment of the patient’s clinical history includes; age at the disease manifestation, ethnic origin/race, hereditary disorder, family history of anemia or jaundice, recently used medications, diet history, and history of liver disease.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2676
content: Cancer of the liver and intrahepatic bile duct manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell carcinoma), which is broadly related to مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ (jaundiced liver disease). This connection indicates that hepatocellular carcinoma often presents within the spectrum of liver diseases that cause jaundice, highlighting the importance of recognizing jaundice as a potential clinical clue to underlying malignant liver pathology. Understanding this relationship aids clinicians in early detection, accurate diagnosis, and appropriate management of patients with liver‑related malignancies and associated jaundice.

title: UMLS -- Run 7 -- Level 2 -- Community 1200
content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is linked to a spectrum of neonatal jaundice conditions and related complications. It is associated with neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation, as well as breast‑milk jaundice, physiologic (newborn) jaundice, and jaundice that is specifically related to preterm birth. In addition, this condition can be accompanied by a loose umbilical cord clamp that leads to bleeding. Recognizing these interconnected manifestations is important for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1081
content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is a complex neonatal condition that gives rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice that results from delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. In addition, the condition includes نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage), which reflects the hemorrhagic component of the disease. Various forms of neonatal jaundice are also linked to this disorder: يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice) in general, يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis of the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (jaundice associated with preterm birth). Together, these interrelated entities illustrate how hemorrhagic and hemolytic processes in the newborn can impair liver function, lead to delayed bilirubin conjugation, and produce respiratory bleeding, underscoring the need for comprehensive assessment and coordinated management of affected infants.

content: Cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct is a malignant condition that manifests as سرطان الخلايا الكبدية (liver cell cancer). Moreover, سرطان الخلايا الكبدية has a broader relationship with مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ, a jaundiced liver disease, suggesting that liver cell cancer can be part of or contribute to the clinical spectrum of jaundice‑related hepatic disorders. Recognizing the interconnections among cancer of liver and intrahepatic bile duct, hepatitis, and مَرَض كَبِدِيّ صَفْراوِيّ is essential for comprehensive diagnosis and management of hepatic pathology.

title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 1096
content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is categorized as a form of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This disorder gives rise to several related manifestations of neonatal jaundice. It is specifically associated with neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation, which reflects a delayed development of the hepatic conjugating system. The same clinical picture is described in Arabic as يرقان نووي and more precisely as يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد. Recognizing that these various terms all refer to the jaundice resulting from Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome and its broader category of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease helps clinicians understand the full spectrum of presentations and guide appropriate patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 937
content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified as a specific form of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN. The broader category of NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN encompasses several distinct types of neonatal jaundice, including Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, the general condition يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice), يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد (neonatal jaundice caused by hemolysis in the fetus or newborn), and يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان (neonatal jaundice associated with preterm birth). These interrelated entities reflect different pathophysiological mechanisms—such as hemolysis, immature hepatic conjugation capacity, and prematurity—that can contribute to hemorrhagic and hemolytic disease in the newborn. Recognizing how Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome fits within this spectrum and how the various forms of neonatal jaundice relate to the overarching disease category is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk assessment, and targeted management of affected infants.

title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 2 -- Community 1288
content: Lucey‑Driscoll syndrome (disorder) is classified within the broader category of neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn. This category also encompasses several distinct forms of neonatal jaundice. One form is neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation that results from the delayed development of the bilirubin‑conjugating system. Additional subtypes include breast‑milk‑induced jaundice (يرقان حليب الثدي), jaundice that arises from immature hepatic conjugation in fetuses or newborns (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد), and jaundice that is associated with preterm birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Together, these conditions represent various manifestations of impaired bilirubin metabolism and hemolysis in the newborn period, highlighting the importance of recognizing specific etiologies for accurate diagnosis and appropriate management.

title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 3 -- Community 2317
content: NEONATAL HEMORRHAGE AND HEMOLYTIC DISEASE OF THE NEWBORN is linked to several serious neonatal complications. It includes نزف سري بعد الولادة (intracranial hemorrhage after birth) and نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ (neonatal respiratory tract hemorrhage) as direct manifestations of the hemorrhagic component. In addition, the condition is associated with يرقان نووي (neonatal jaundice). The neonatal jaundice observed in this context is specifically described as Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation and further clarified as Neonatal jaundice due to delayed conjugation from delayed development of the conjugating system, indicating that the immature hepatic conjugation pathway in the newborn leads to accumulation of unconjugated bilirubin. Recognizing the interplay between hemorrhagic events, respiratory bleeding, and delayed‑conjugation jaundice is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2429
content: Neonatal hemorrhage and hemolytic disease of the newborn is a complex neonatal condition that can give rise to several related complications. One important manifestation is neonatal jaundice caused by delayed conjugation due to the immature development of the hepatic conjugating system. The disorder may also be accompanied by post‑natal umbilical bleeding (نزف سري بعد الولادة) and bleeding from the neonatal respiratory tract (نَزْف المسالك التنفسية الوَليدِيّ). In addition, the disease can produce a form of fetal‑origin jaundice (يرقان نووي ناجم عن التمنيع الإسوي لدى الأجنة أو المواليد) and a jaundice that is specifically linked to premature birth (يرقان وليدي مرتبط بالولادة قبل الأوان). Recognizing these child conditions is essential for comprehensive assessment and management of affected newborns.
